verses:
aṅga⋅śyāmalima⋅chaṭābhir abhito mandī⋅kṛtendīvaraṁ
jāḍyam⋅jāguḍa⋅rociṣām⋅vidadhatam⋅paṭṭāmbarasya śriyā
vṛndāraṇya⋅vilāsinam⋅hṛdi lasad⋅dāmābhir āmodaraṁ
rādhā⋅skandha⋅niveśitojjvala⋅bhujam⋅dhyāyema dāmodaram

phullendīvara⋅kāntim indu⋅vadanaṁ varhāvataṁsa⋅priyaṁ
śrīvatsāṅkam udāra⋅kaustubha⋅dharaṁ pītāmbaraṁ sundaram
gopīnāṁ nayanotpalārcita⋅tanuṁ go⋅gopa⋅saṅghāvṛtaṁ
govindaṁ kala⋅veṇu⋅vādana⋅paraṁ divyāṅga⋅bhūṣaṁ bhaje

kasturī⋅tilakaṁ lalāṭa⋅paṭale vakṣaḥ⋅sthale kaustubhaṁ
nāsāgre vara⋅mauktikaṁ kara⋅tale veṇuḥ kare kaṅkaṇam
sarvāṅge hari⋅candanaṁ sulalitaṁ kaṇṭhe ca muktāvalī
gopa⋅strī⋅pariveṣṭito vijayate gopāla⋅cūḍāmaṇiḥ

varhāpīḍaṁ naṭa⋅vara⋅vapuḥ karṇayoḥ karṇikāraṁ
vibhrad vāsaḥ kanaka⋅kapiśaṁ vaijayantīṁ ca mālām
randhrān veṇor adhara⋅sudhayāpūrayan gopa⋅vṛndair
vṛndāraṇyaṁ sva⋅pada⋅ramaṇaṁ prāviśad gīta⋅kīrtiḥ

śyāmaṁ hiraṇya⋅paridhiṁ vanamālya⋅varha⋅
dhātu⋅pravāla⋅naṭa⋅veṣam anavratāṁse
vinyasta⋅hastam itareṇa dhunānam avjaṁ
karṇotpalālaka⋅kapola⋅mukhābja⋅hāsam

naumīḍya te’bhra⋅vapuṣe taḍid⋅ambarāya
guñjāvataṁsa⋅paripiccha⋅lasan⋅mukhāya
vanya⋅sraje kavala⋅vetra⋅viṣāṇa⋅veṇu⋅
lakṣma⋅śriye mṛdu⋅pade paśupāṅgajāya

athāpi te deva padāmbuja⋅dvaya⋅
prasāda⋅leśānugṛhīta eva hi
jānāti tattvaṁ bhagavan⋅mahimno
na cānya eko’pi ciraṁ vicinvan

tad astu me nātha sa bhūri⋅bhāgo
bhave’tra vānyatra tu vā tiraścām
jenāham eko’pi bhavaj⋅janānāṁ
bhūtvā niṣeve tava pāda⋅pallavam

jūyaṁ nṛ⋅loke bata bhūri⋅bhāgā
lokaṁ punānā munayo’bhiyanti
jeṣāṁ gṛhān āvasatīti sākṣād
gūḍhaṁ paraṁ brahma manuṣya⋅liṅgam

śyāma⋅sundara śikhaṇḍa⋅śekhara
smera⋅hāsa muralī⋅manohara
rādhikā⋅rasika māṁ kṛpā⋅nidhe
sva⋅priyā⋅caraṇa⋅kiṅkarīṁ kuru

aho bhāgyam aho bhāgyaṁ
nanda⋅gopa⋅vrajaukasām
jan⋅mitraṁ paramānandaṁ
pūrṇaṁ brahma sanātanam

nandaḥ kim akarod brahman
śreya evaṁ mahodayam
jaśodā ca mahā⋅bhāgā
papau jasyāḥ stanaṁ hariḥ
en_translation:
We meditate on Dāmodara, whose body's dark luster completely dims the blue lotus; who by the splendor of His silken garment puts to shame the brilliance of saffron; who sports in Vṛndāvana; who is delightful with shining ropes around His belly; and whose radiant arm is placed on Rādhā's shoulder.

I worship Govinda, whose splendor is like a blooming blue lotus, whose face is like the moon, who loves a peacock feather ornament; who bears the Śrīvatsa mark, wears the large Kaustubha jewel, is dressed in yellow garments, and is beautiful. His body is worshiped by the lotus-like eyes of the gopīs, and He is surrounded by cows and cowherds. He is dedicated to playing the sweet flute and has divine bodily ornaments.

A musk tilaka on His forehead, a Kaustubha on His chest; a fine pearl on the tip of His nose, a flute in His palm, and a bracelet on His wrist. Hari-candana gracefully applied all over His body, and a pearl necklace around His neck. Surrounded by the gopīs, the crest-jewel of cowherds is victorious.

He entered Vṛndāvana, which is charming with His own footprints, His glories being sung by the cowherd boys. He wore a peacock feather on His head, His body like that of a fine dancer, with karṇikāra flowers in His ears. He wore garments of golden-tawny color and a Vaijayantī garland, filling the holes of His flute with the nectar of His lips.

I remember Kṛṣṇa, who is dark-complexioned, wearing golden garments, a forest garland, and peacock feathers. Dressed like a dancer with mineral colors and new leaves, with one hand placed on the shoulder of His friend. With the other, He twirls a lotus. His lotus-like face is smiling, with lotus-like ears, locks of hair, and cheeks.

I praise You, O adorable One, whose body is like a cloud, dressed in garments like lightning. Whose face shines with an ornament of guñjā and a peacock feather. Adorned with a forest garland, carrying a morsel of food, a stick, a horn, and a flute. Whose beauty is characterized by these emblems, with soft feet, the son of the cowherd.

Yet, O Lord, only one who is graced by a trace of the mercy of Your two lotus feet truly knows the truth of Your greatness, O Lord, and no one else, even by long deliberation alone.

Therefore, O Lord, may that great fortune be mine, in this birth, or in any other birth, even as an animal; by which I, becoming even one of Your devotees, may serve Your lotus feet.

Oh, you are indeed most fortunate among human beings in this world! The sages approach you, purifying the world; in whose homes resides directly the hidden Supreme Brahman in human form.

O Śyāmasundara! O Peacock-feathered One! O One with a smiling face! O Enchanter with the flute! O Connoisseur of Rādhikā! O Ocean of Mercy! Please make me a maidservant of the feet of Your beloved.

Oh, the great fortune, the great fortune of the residents of Nanda's Vraja! Whose friend is the Supreme Bliss, the complete Brahman, the Eternal.

What pious deeds did Nanda perform, O brāhmaṇa, to attain such exceedingly great prosperity? And the greatly fortunate Yaśodā, from whose breast Hari drank milk.
